content:
First, label the elements of \(M\) by their index \(i\).  One checks easily that the \(i\)–th element of \(M\) is

\[
M_i=\begin{cases}
\;(3m+1)&\text{if }i=2m+1\text{ is odd,}\\
\;(3m+2)&\text{if }i=2m+2\text{ is even.}
\end{cases}
\]

We look for \(2n\) consecutive terms
\[
M_a+M_{a+1}+\cdots+M_{a+2n-1}=300.
\]
There are two alignment‐cases (depending on whether \(a\) is odd or even), and in each case one finds that the sum boils down to an equation of the form
\[
3\,n\,(2k+n)\;=\;300
\quad\text{or}\quad
3\,n\,(2k+n-1)\;=\;300,
\]
both of which reduce to finding factor–pairs of \(100\).  Checking parity‐and‐positivity constraints on the auxiliary integer \(k\) forces
\[
n\in\{1,2,4,5,10\}.
\]
One checks directly, for each of these \(n\), that there is indeed a run of \(2n\) consecutive elements of \(M\) summing to 300.  

Answer:  
\(\boxed{1,2,4,5,10}\).
